content.text: When an organisation decides to add a GenAI capability — a document Q&A system, a coding assistant, an automated support tier — it faces a structural choice: build the solution internally, buy a commercial product, or engage a partner to integrate a third-party model into an existing workflow. Each path has a different cost structure across time. Total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis makes these costs comparable so the decision is based on multi-year financial reality rather than the Year 1 price tag or the appeal of technical ownership.
Why TCO Differs from Procurement Price

The price a vendor quotes — or the engineering estimate for building — captures only a fraction of the true cost. TCO includes categories that procurement and engineering teams systematically underestimate:

After a solution is built or purchased, someone must run it. For a built solution, this is engineering time for on-call support, incident response, dependency upgrades, and scaling. For a purchased SaaS product, operational cost shifts to the vendor, but integration maintenance — keeping the organisation's systems connected to the vendor's evolving APIs — remains internal.

Engineering time spent building and maintaining a GenAI solution is time not spent on the organisation's core product. For most companies, building infrastructure is not a competitive advantage — the opportunity cost of a senior ML engineer spending two quarters on infrastructure tooling is two quarters of product work not done.

A built solution accumulates internal dependencies — custom data pipelines, fine-tuned models, proprietary evaluation frameworks. Replacing it later is expensive. A purchased solution that becomes embedded in core workflows also has switching cost: migrating vendor-specific features, retraining users, and re-doing integrations. TCO analysis for a 3-year window must account for the probability that the decision will need to be revisited.

GDPR DPAs, security questionnaires, SOC 2 reviews, and privacy impact assessments all take time and money. For a build path, these apply to internally hosted models and data pipelines. For a buy path, they apply to the vendor relationship. These costs are rarely included in engineering estimates or vendor quotes.

A TCO model for a GenAI solution should cover five cost categories across a 3-year projection. Three years is the minimum horizon: Year 1 costs are dominated by initial setup; Year 2 reflects steady-state operations; Year 3 shows whether the solution's cost trajectory is stable, rising, or declining.
4 senior engineers × 6 months + ML engineer × full year
0.5–1 FTE ongoing; increases Y2 as usage grows
Vector DB, object storage, observability stack
Typical enterprise SaaS pricing: 20–40% uplift at renewal
Initial integration heavy; maintenance reduces over time
System integrator project; fixed-price or T&M
Licence for the underlying model the partner integrates
Retainer for updates, new features, incident support
Internal PM + technical lead time; cannot fully outsource
Both partner and underlying vendor reviewed

TCO tables are only as good as the assumptions behind them. Engineering salary assumptions, usage growth rates, and SaaS renewal uplift rates can shift the 3-year total by 30–50%. The model's value is in making assumptions explicit and comparing paths on a consistent basis, not in producing a precise forecast.
Computing the TCO Model in Code

# tco/rag_platform_example.py

build = TCOScenario(name="Build", components=[
    TCOComponent("Engineering: initial build",     600_000,       0,       0),
    TCOComponent("Engineering: ops & maintenance", 150_000, 200_000, 200_000),
    TCOComponent("Engineering: features",          100_000, 200_000, 150_000),
    TCOComponent("Infrastructure: compute",         80_000, 120_000, 140_000),
    TCOComponent("Infrastructure: storage/network", 20_000,  30_000,  35_000),
    TCOComponent("Compliance",                      30_000,  10_000,  10_000),
])

buy = TCOScenario(name="Buy", components=[
    TCOComponent("Licence / subscription",         300_000, 420_000, 500_000),
    TCOComponent("API usage costs",                 80_000, 150_000, 200_000),
    TCOComponent("Integration engineering",        120_000,  60_000,  40_000),
    TCOComponent("Internal tooling",                40_000,  50_000,  60_000),
    TCOComponent("Compliance: vendor DD",           20_000,   8_000,   8_000),
])

partner = TCOScenario(name="Partner", components=[
    TCOComponent("Partner: integration build",     350_000,       0,       0),
    TCOComponent("Underlying model licence",       200_000, 280_000, 320_000),
    TCOComponent("Partner: support retainer",       80_000, 120_000, 140_000),
    TCOComponent("Internal: oversight",             60_000,  80_000,  80_000),
    TCOComponent("Compliance",                      30_000,  10_000,  10_000),
])

for scenario in [build, buy, partner]:
    s = scenario.summary()
    print(f"\n{s['scenario']}")
    print(f"  Year 1: ${s['year1']:,.0f}")
    print(f"  Year 2: ${s['year2']:,.0f}")
    print(f"  Year 3: ${s['year3']:,.0f}")
    print(f"  3-year: ${s['3yr_total']:,.0f}")

# Output:
# Build
#   Year 1: $980,000
#   Year 2: $560,000
#   Year 3: $535,000
#   3-year: $2,075,000
#
# Buy
#   Year 1: $560,000
#   Year 2: $688,000
#   Year 3: $808,000
#   3-year: $2,056,000
#
# Partner
#   Year 1: $720,000
#   Year 2: $490,000
#   Year 3: $550,000
#   3-year: $1,760,000
Qualitative Factors That Adjust the Decision

The TCO model provides a financial ranking, but the cheapest option is not always the right option. Several qualitative factors can shift the decision even when the numbers point in a different direction.
Data cannot leave the organisation's own infrastructure due to regulatory or contractual constraints
Vendor offers a private deployment or data residency option that satisfies constraints
Partner deploys within the organisation's cloud account under the organisation's data controls
The GenAI capability is a core product differentiator that competitors should not be able to replicate by purchasing the same tool
Capability is operational (support bot, document summarisation) — not a competitive differentiator
Differentiation comes from domain expertise the partner provides, not from the underlying model
Organisation has existing ML infrastructure and experienced team; build is faster than procurement
SaaS product can be live in weeks; internal build is 6–12 months away from MVP
Partner can deliver faster than internal build; slower than pure SaaS but with more customisation
Build avoids lock-in but creates internal lock-in to the chosen technology stack
SaaS lock-in is real: proprietary data formats, API-specific features, pricing leverage at renewal
Lock-in to both partner and underlying model; diversification across two vendors is also two failure points
Organisation has a capable ML team; build maintains and grows that capability
ML team is thin or non-existent; building would require hiring first
Partner brings ML capability the organisation lacks; risk is that capability never transfers internally

TCO models are sensitive to assumptions. Before presenting a TCO analysis to leadership, identify the two or three assumptions that have the most impact on the 3-year total and vary them to show the range of outcomes.

# tco/sensitivity.py

def sensitivity_analysis(base_scenario: TCOScenario, component_name: str,
                          low_factor: float, high_factor: float) -> dict:
    """
    Vary a single cost component by low_factor and high_factor and report
    the impact on the 3-year TCO total.
    """
    base_total = base_scenario.total
    results = {}

    for label, factor in [("low", low_factor), ("base", 1.0), ("high", high_factor)]:
        adjusted = 0
        for c in base_scenario.components:
            if c.name == component_name:
                adjusted += c.total * factor
            else:
                adjusted += c.total
        results[label] = {"total": adjusted, "delta_vs_base": adjusted - base_total}

    return {
        "scenario":  base_scenario.name,
        "component": component_name,
        "results":   results,
    }

# Example: how sensitive is the Buy scenario to SaaS renewal uplift?
# Base assumes 20% uplift (Y2) and 40% uplift (Y3).
# Low: 10% uplift. High: 50% uplift.
result = sensitivity_analysis(buy, "Licence / subscription",
                              low_factor=0.80, high_factor=1.25)
# Low:  3-year = $1.87M  (−$186k vs base)
# Base: 3-year = $2.06M
# High: 3-year = $2.31M  (+$253k vs base)
#
# A 50% uplift scenario makes Buy more expensive than Build over 3 years.
# This is a material decision factor that should be surfaced to leadership.

A TCO analysis presented as a spreadsheet of numbers is rarely persuasive. The analysis should tell a story: what the organisation gets for each path, what the key risks are, and what the recommendation is. Senior stakeholders want to understand the key assumptions and the recommendation — not audit every line item.
State upfront which path is recommended and why. The TCO table supports the recommendation; it is not the recommendation itself.

If the cheapest Year 1 option is not the cheapest 3-year option (as in the Build example, where Year 1 is most expensive but Year 3 is cheapest), make the crossover explicit. A chart showing cumulative spend over 36 months makes this more intuitive than a table.
Call out the assumptions that, if wrong, would change the recommendation. If the Buy path is recommended but SaaS renewal uplift is the critical assumption, say so explicitly.

Each path has a distinct risk profile. Build risks: capability gap, longer time to production, team attrition. Buy risks: price escalation at renewal, feature gaps, vendor stability. Partner risks: dependency on external team, slow change velocity. State these alongside the numbers.

Even teams that attempt a structured TCO analysis routinely omit certain cost categories, which causes the Build path to appear cheaper than it is and the Buy path to appear more expensive than its base subscription price.
Include as a separate line: prompt evaluation framework, golden dataset curation, regression test infrastructure
Estimate weeks of prompt engineering per capability area; multiply by loaded headcount cost
Training materials, workshops, internal champions programme; often absorbed into general overhead and never costed
GenAI systems have novel failure modes (hallucination, latency spikes, context length edge cases); on-call is more expensive than for deterministic software
When vendors deprecate model versions, existing prompts may need re-tuning; this is a recurring cost for any Buy path that pins model versions
Audit logging, output sampling, human review workflows; required for regulated use cases but often assumed to be 'just logging'
Build: $100–300k to replace custom infra; Buy: $30–100k to migrate data and re-integrate
Include as a probability-weighted cost: P(exit in 3 years) × exit effort; most organisations exit or significantly change GenAI solutions within 5 years

When two paths have similar 3-year totals, the break-even analysis identifies the point at which the initially more expensive path becomes the cheaper option on a cumulative basis. This is particularly relevant when comparing Build (high Year 1 cost, declining subsequent costs) against Buy (low Year 1 cost, rising renewal costs).

# tco/breakeven.py

def cumulative_costs(scenario: TCOScenario, months: int = 36) -> list[float]:
    """
    Interpolate annual costs to monthly and compute cumulative spend.
    Assumes costs are distributed evenly within each year.
    """
    annual = [scenario.year_total(1), scenario.year_total(2), scenario.year_total(3)]
    monthly = []
    for yr_cost in annual:
        monthly.extend([yr_cost / 12] * 12)
    monthly = monthly[:months]

    cumulative = []
    running = 0
    for m in monthly:
        running += m
        cumulative.append(round(running))
    return cumulative

def find_breakeven(scenario_a: TCOScenario, scenario_b: TCOScenario) -> int | None:
    """
    Find the month at which scenario_b becomes cheaper than scenario_a
    on a cumulative basis. Returns None if no crossover within 36 months.
    """
    cum_a = cumulative_costs(scenario_a)
    cum_b = cumulative_costs(scenario_b)
    for month, (a, b) in enumerate(zip(cum_a, cum_b), start=1):
        if b < a:
            return month
    return None

# Example with the scenarios from earlier:
# Build is more expensive in Year 1 ($980k vs $560k for Buy),
# but cheaper in Year 3 ($535k vs $808k for Buy).
# Find the crossover month:
crossover = find_breakeven(buy, build)
if crossover:
    print(f"Build becomes cheaper than Buy at month {crossover}")
    # Output: Build becomes cheaper than Buy at month 27
    # → The Build path pays back its higher upfront cost 27 months in,
    #   given these assumptions. If the planning horizon is < 27 months,
    #   Buy is the better financial decision.
else:
    print("Build never becomes cheaper than Buy within 36 months")

The break-even month is a useful executive communication tool: it converts the abstract "3-year TCO comparison" into a concrete threshold. If the organisation's planning horizon (contract term, product roadmap horizon, expected organisational stability) extends past the break-even, the initially more expensive path is justified. If not, it is not — regardless of what happens in Year 3.
Updating the TCO Model Over Time

A TCO model written at procurement time becomes inaccurate within months. Usage grows faster or slower than projected, vendor pricing changes, and the internal team's productivity assumptions prove wrong. The model should be updated at two milestones:

After the first year of operation, replace Year 1 projected figures with actuals from Finance, the engineering team's time tracking system, and vendor invoices. Reproject Years 2 and 3 based on the observed cost trajectory. If actuals differ from projections by more than 20%, revisit the decision.

Before any major contract renewal or budget cycle, update the model with current vendor pricing, current headcount costs, and current usage data. A tool that was the right choice at a $50k/year subscription may not be the right choice at $200k/year after three rounds of renewal uplift.

A TCO analysis done at procurement and never revisited is a snapshot, not a model. The value of TCO is in the ongoing comparison between projected and actual costs — that comparison is what enables the organisation to make a correction before it is locked in.

TCO analysis for GenAI solutions extends beyond the initial licence or engineering estimate to include operational costs, opportunity costs, switching costs, and compliance overhead across a 3-year horizon. The Build path has the highest Year 1 cost but declining subsequent years; the Buy path has the lowest Year 1 cost but rising renewal pressure; the Partner path sits in between and depends heavily on the partner's retainer pricing. A structured TCO model with explicit assumptions makes these cost trajectories comparable. Sensitivity analysis on the key assumptions — particularly SaaS renewal uplift and engineering headcount — shows whether the numerical ranking is robust or fragile. Qualitative factors (data sensitivity, capability differentiation, speed to production, lock-in risk) adjust the recommendation when the numbers are close or when financial cost is not the only relevant consideration.
